name: Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat
starting_point: Costa d'en Blanes urbanization. Drive to the top, park at the end of Calle de la Televisión, continue on Camí des Ri de ses Creus to a V-junction, take the right branch toward Coll del Pastors
best_season: Any, though avoid summer if possible
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 461100 E, 4379715 N. DATUM ETRS89: 461193 E, 4379919 N
equipment: One 40 m rope, 2 hangers for the two bolts, sling for the rebelay on rock bridge
description: A 38 m deep predominantly vertical cave on the south slope of Puig de Na Boira, explored in January 2010. The 4x6 m entrance leads to a 22 m long chamber illuminated by natural light, with red earth and scattered formations. The descent continues through an extremely narrow fracture lined with flowstone, requiring removal of equipment at the tightest point. A dry stone wall near the entrance was built as an ancient shelter, and the cave is rated as a site of speleological interest.
full_description:
Located on the southern slope of Puig de Na Boira, this chasm was discovered by J. V. Pardo. Together with the neighboring cavity "Escletxa dets Ossos," the Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat has a different morphology depending on which area we are talking about, since the most external part of it, and always in my opinion, is different from the fracture zone where most of the chasm develops.

Once J. V. Pardo completed the clearing work, all that remained was to enter its depths to carry out the exploration work, which happened on January 31, 2010, in the company of members of the speleological group VOLTORS (Palma). With the cave explored, its survey still remained pending. For this purpose, on May 5, 2010, we set to work on the survey, dividing the work into two groups so that the deepest and most demanding part of the cavity was handled by R. Minguillón and J. V. Pardo, and the rest of the cavity by yours truly accompanied by L. Morante.

The entrance, mostly hidden by bushes, is about 4 x 6 meters. Through it we will comfortably access the interior of the cavity, composed of a chamber about 22 meters long and a little more than half a dozen meters wide; the maximum height does not exceed 5 meters. The outside light illuminates the interior of the chamber almost entirely, allowing one to appreciate the intense red color of the earth which, accompanied by rocks of different sizes, forms parts of the walls as well as the ceiling. There are not many formations to be found in this area of the chasm, and those that exist are in the most western part of the chamber, formed by small stalactites, the occasional curtain, as well as wall and floor flowstones. Also noteworthy is the abundant wall concretion in the eastern area, the place where the cave continues.

The crack through which we begin the descent does not exceed one meter in width at its widest part. The walls are draped in wall flowstones and it is manageable without vertical progression equipment down to -10 meters, where we will set up the rope using two small stalagmites that are at a little more than half a meter in height. The narrowest part of this already narrow cavity is found right at this point, since to access the small ledge about five meters below, depending on the build of the speleologist, it may be necessary to remove part of the vertical progression equipment to get through this extremely tight passage. A curious anecdote happened to us at this location. Once past the narrow passage and as I was saying before, we will position ourselves on a small ledge of approximately two meters where the ground, covered with damp earth, is slippery and can be dangerous if we are not attached to our rope. The rest of the cavity follows the vertical fracture where, hidden among a chaos of rocks, small stalactites develop as well as curtains, some of them of considerable size. In the deepest part of the cavity, rocks abound as well as earth that in some cases covers them; some bone remains are also found in this place. Wall flowstones are abundant throughout the entire descent since the walls remain covered by a thin film of water.

Inside the cavity and near the entrance, in a corner, the construction of a dry stone wall conditioned this place to serve as shelter and protect from the inclement weather those who once used it. This particular area was possibly used because venturing into the western zone caused some fear due to the possibility of ceiling collapse.

Perhaps the cave may not be enough to occupy a full day, but that should not worry us since in the surroundings we have enough cavities to keep us busy for more than one day, notably the "Cova/avenc de na Picacento," "Sa Escletxa dets Ossos" which is found just over a dozen meters away, "Cova de ses Cabres," "Avenc des Xuclamel" located near the summit of Puig de Na Boira, and why not the "Cova del Ferro" which we pass on the way back.

The Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat is considered a site of speleological interest.
